source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure Logging is enabled for HTTP(S) Load Balancer

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure Logging is enabled for HTTP(S) Load Balancer
input_row.description: Logging enabled on a HTTPS Load Balancer will show all network traffic and its destination.
input_row.rationale: Logging will allow you to view HTTPS network traffic to your web applications. Impact: On high use systems with a high percentage sample rate, the logging file may grow to high capacity in a short amount of time. Ensure that the sample rate is set appropriately so that storage costs are not exorbitant.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. From Google Cloud home open the Navigation Menu in the top left. 2. Under the Networking heading select Network services. 3. Select the HTTPS load-balancer you wish to audit. 4. Select Edit then Backend Configuration. 5. Select Edit on the corresponding backend service. 6. Ensure that Enable Logging is selected. Also ensure that Sample Rate is set to an appropriate level for your needs. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Run the following command gcloud compute backend-services describe <serviceName> 1. Ensure that enable-logging is enabled and sample rate is set to your desired level.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. From Google Cloud home open the Navigation Menu in the top left. 2. Under the Networking heading select Network services. 3. Select the HTTPS load-balancer you wish to audit. 4. Select Edit then Backend Configuration. 5. Select Edit on the corresponding backend service. 6. Click Enable Logging. 7. Set Sample Rate to a desired value. This is a percentage as a decimal point. 1.0 is 100%. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Run the following command gcloud compute backend-services update <serviceName> --region=REGION -- enable-logging --logging-sample-rate=<percentageAsADecimal> Default Value: By default logging for https load balancing is disabled. When logging is enabled it sets the default sample rate as 1.0 or 100%. Ensure this value fits the need of your organization to avoid high storage costs. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/ 2. https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/https/https-logging- monitoring#gcloud:-global-mode 3. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/backend-services/
input_row.impact: On high use systems with a high percentage sample rate, the logging file may grow to high capacity in a short amount of time. Ensure that the sample rate is set appropriately so that storage costs are not exorbitant.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be fully verified using GCP APIs or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands.
- The audit examples already indicate the use of `gcloud compute backend-services describe` to verify if logging is enabled and to check the sample rate.
- This is a standard property of the GCP HTTP(S) Load Balancer, a service that is exposed and manageable via the Google Cloud SDK and CLI.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_backend_service_https_logging_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command to obtain details for backend services related to HTTPS load balancers.
- Specifically, use `gcloud compute backend-services describe <serviceName> --region=<REGION>` to check the `logConfig` property.
- Enumerate through all backend services for each region and load balancer to verify:
  - `logConfig.enable` is set to `true`.
  - `logConfig.sampleRate` is set to a desired and appropriate value according to organization policies.
- Pass Criteria:
  - Logging is enabled (`logConfig.enable` is `true`).
  - Sample rate (`logConfig.sampleRate`) fits the organization's needs and compliance requirements to avoid high storage costs.
- If `logConfig.enable` is not found or false, or if the sample rate is inappropriate, it's a Fail.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. This control can be fully assessed using GCP APIs or the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI. There is no need for manual validation unless there are exceptions or unsupported resources.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Coverage:**  
  The `gcloud compute backend-services describe` command exposes the `logConfig` property, which is the authoritative source for logging configuration on backend services used by HTTP(S) Load Balancers.  
  Enumerating all backend services via `gcloud compute backend-services list` and then describing each is a standard, scriptable approach.
- **Other Considerations:**  
  There is no equivalent to AWS CloudFormation in GCP for this resource, but GCP Config Validator or Forseti could also be used for policy-as-code checks. The assessment correctly focuses on gcloud/API methods.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 9/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The use of `gcloud compute backend-services describe <serviceName> --region=<REGION>` is correct for regional backend services. For global backend services (common for HTTP(S) Load Balancers), the command should be `gcloud compute backend-services describe <serviceName> --global`.
  - The logic to enumerate all backend services and check `logConfig.enable` and `logConfig.sampleRate` is correct.
  - Pass/fail criteria are accurate.
- **Program Name:**  
  - The program name `gcp_compute_backend_service_https_logging_enabled` is descriptive, but could be more precise (see Improvements).
- **Minor Issues:**  
  - The assessment does not mention global backend services, which are typical for HTTP(S) Load Balancers.
  - The sample rate check is left open-ended ("desired and appropriate value"), which may lead to inconsistent enforcement unless organizational policy is referenced.
  - No mention of filtering only backend services used by HTTP(S) Load Balancers (vs. TCP/UDP or internal load balancers).

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Global vs. Regional Backend Services:**  
  - Most HTTP(S) Load Balancers use global backend services. The assessment should explicitly state that both regional and global backend services must be checked, using `--global` and `--region` flags as appropriate.
- **Filtering for HTTP(S) Load Balancers:**  
  - Not all backend services are necessarily attached to HTTP(S) Load Balancers. The audit should ideally filter backend services to only those attached to HTTP(S) Load Balancers, which can be done by cross-referencing forwarding rules and target proxies.
- **Sample Rate Policy:**  
  - The sample rate check should reference a specific organizational policy or CIS recommendation (e.g., "sampleRate >= 0.5").
- **Alternative API Methods:**  
  - The REST API equivalent is `GET https://compute.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/{project}/global/backendServices/{backendService}`.
  - For large environments, using the REST API or gcloud with `--format=json` for scripting is recommended.
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - For clarity and alignment with naming conventions, consider:  
    `gcp_compute_backend_service_https_logging_enabled_and_sample_rate`  
    or  
    `gcp_compute_backend_service_https_log_config_enforced`
- **Documentation:**  
  - Recommend including example gcloud commands for both global and regional resources.
  - Suggest documenting how to map backend services to HTTP(S) Load Balancers for more targeted checks.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The approach is technically sound and aligns with GCP best practices and CIS guidance.
- Minor improvements are needed for completeness and precision, but the core logic is correct.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Modify** the automation details to:
  - Explicitly include both global and regional backend services.
  - Reference organizational policy or CIS for sample rate.
  - Suggest filtering backend services to only those attached to HTTP(S) Load Balancers.
  - Update the program name for clarity and specificity.
- Otherwise, the assessment is fundamentally correct and can be kept with these enhancements.

---

**Summary Table**

| Section                | Assessment        |
|------------------------|------------------|
| Approach Validation    | AGREE            |
| Technical Accuracy     | 9/10             |
| Improvements           | See above        |
| Confidence             | HIGH             |
| Final Recommendation   | Modify           |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that the logging configuration for HTTP(S) Load Balancer backend services is fully exposed via the GCP API and can be audited using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI. The `logConfig` property, including both `enable` and `sampleRate`, is available for all backend services (regional and global). There is no requirement for manual inspection, as all necessary data is programmatically accessible and scriptable.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_compute_backend_service_https_logging_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** Compute Engine Backend Services (used by HTTP(S) Load Balancers)
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - Enumerate all backend services using:  
    - `gcloud compute backend-services list --global`  
    - `gcloud compute backend-services list --regions=<region>` (for regional services, if applicable)
  - For each backend service, describe it using:  
    - `gcloud compute backend-services describe <SERVICE_NAME> --global`  
    - or `gcloud compute backend-services describe <SERVICE_NAME> --region=<REGION>`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - `logConfig.enable` (should be `true`)
  - `logConfig.sampleRate` (should be set to an appropriate value per organizational policy)
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - `logConfig.enable` is `true` for all backend services associated with HTTP(S) Load Balancers.
  - `logConfig.sampleRate` is set and meets the organization's defined threshold for logging.
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any backend service has `logConfig.enable` as `false` or missing.
  - `logConfig.sampleRate` is unset or outside acceptable organizational parameters.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no substantive disagreements; both AI opinions supported automation. The only minor technical note from the second opinion (regarding global vs. regional backend services) is incorporated in the automation details for completeness.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
